questions.question: How do you add a new embed block to a Notion page?

options.text: Click the + that appears to the left margin and choose Embed.
options.explanation: The help center explicitly states to click the + that appears on the left when hovering over a new line, then choose Embed.

options.text: Drag and drop a file directly from your computer into the page.
options.explanation: Drag and drop is used for resizing or moving existing embeds, not for creating a new one from scratch.

options.text: Copy the embed code from the external site and paste it as plain text.
options.explanation: You should paste the URL or embed link and select 'Paste as bookmark' or 'Create Embed', not raw code.

options.text: Type /embed in the command menu to open a file upload dialog.
options.explanation: The /embed command is used to upload and display your own image, audio, video, or file, not to create a generic embed block.

questions.question: Which file types are supported for embedding in Notion?

options.text: HTML, PDF, Audio, Video, and Images.
options.explanation: The article explicitly lists these five types as supported for embedding.

options.text: DOCX, XLSX, PPTX, and PDF.
options.explanation: While PDF is supported, Word, Excel, and PowerPoint are not listed as supported file types for embedding.

options.text: EXE, BAT, and SYS files.
options.explanation: Executable files are not supported for embedding; only specific media types like HTML, PDF, Audio, Video, and Images are.

options.text: Only Images and Videos.
options.explanation: The list includes more than just images and videos, specifically HTML, PDF, and Audio.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Link Mention and a Web Bookmark?

options.text: Link mentions appear inline and subtle, while bookmarks appear as distinct blocks with title and description.
options.explanation: The article explains that link mentions are inline like text and more subtle, whereas bookmarks appear as distinct blocks with the page title and URL.

options.text: Link mentions require a login to the external site, while bookmarks do not.
options.explanation: Neither is explicitly described as requiring a login; the difference lies primarily in their visual appearance and layout.

options.text: Link mentions are for internal links only, while bookmarks are for external links.
options.explanation: Both types are used to incorporate web links into pages, regardless of whether they are internal or external.

options.text: Link mentions are blue and bookmarks are green.
options.explanation: The article does not specify color coding for these link types; the distinction is based on their layout and subtlety.

questions.question: You receive a 'Failed to Load' error when trying to embed a specific website. Why is this likely happening?

options.text: The website prohibits their content from being embedded elsewhere.
options.explanation: The article notes that some websites prohibit their content from being embedded elsewhere, which results in a Failed to Load error.

options.text: You are using the desktop app instead of the browser.
options.explanation: Embeds that require login to an external site will not work on the desktop or mobile apps, but the error message here specifically points to site restrictions.

options.text: Your internet connection is too slow to load the iframe.
options.explanation: While slow internet could cause loading issues, the specific error and the context of 'some websites prohibit their content' point to the site's embedding policy.

options.text: The embed link you pasted started with 'https' instead of 'http'.
options.explanation: The instructions state the link should start with https, not that this causes a Failed to Load error specifically.

questions.question: How can you resize an embedded block in Notion?

options.text: Drag the black bars that appear on the sides of the embed.
options.explanation: The documentation states that most embeds can be resized by hovering and dragging the black bars that appear on the sides.

options.text: Click the corner handle and drag to resize.
options.explanation: The corner handle is used to move the embed, not resize it. The black bars on the sides are specifically for resizing.

options.text: Double-click the embed to open a resize menu.
options.explanation: There is no double-click resize menu; resizing is done via the black bars on the sides.

options.text: Use the keyboard arrow keys to adjust the width.
options.explanation: Notion does not support resizing via keyboard arrows; the drag-and-drop method with black bars is required.

questions.question: What is a requirement for embedding a Google Calendar into a Notion page?

options.text: The Gmail address used for the calendar must be the same as the one used for your Notion account.
options.explanation: The article specifies that you can embed a view-only Google Calendar into Notion if the Gmail address for the calendar is the same one used for Notion.

options.text: You must use the embed code provided by Google, not the URL.
options.explanation: You copy the 'Public URL' from the Google Calendar settings and paste that URL into Notion to create the embed.

options.text: You must be on an Enterprise Plan to embed Google Calendars.
options.explanation: There is no mention of an Enterprise requirement; the requirement is simply matching email addresses.

options.text: You cannot embed Google Calendars, only Google Docs or Sheets.
options.explanation: The article explicitly describes how to embed a Google Calendar.

questions.question: When integrating Zoom into Notion for the first time, what step is required?

options.text: You must authenticate the app by clicking 'Connect to Zoom'.
options.explanation: The article states that you have to authenticate the app the very first time you paste a link, specifically by clicking the 'Connect to Zoom to update' button.

options.text: You must download the Zoom desktop application on your computer.
options.explanation: The integration works within the browser or Notion app; you do not need to download a desktop app to connect.

options.text: You must paste the link as a bookmark rather than a mention.
options.explanation: The article allows you to choose between 'Paste as mention' or 'Paste as preview' after authentication.

options.text: You must share the Notion page with Zoom to enable the feature.
options.explanation: Authentication happens locally via the 'Connect to Zoom' button, not via sharing settings.

questions.question: How can you open the original source website of an embedded link?

options.text: Hover over the embed and click 'Original' or 'View Original' in the context menu.
options.explanation: The article outlines three ways: clicking 'Original' on hover, or using the '⋮⋮' icon or '•••' menu and selecting 'View Original'.

options.text: Right-click the embed and select 'Open in new tab'.
options.explanation: While you can generally right-click to open in a new tab, the specific Notion feature to jump to source is 'Original' or 'View Original'.

options.text: Click the 'Go to Source' button in the top right corner of the embed.
options.explanation: There is no 'Go to Source' button; the action is accessed via the 'Original' text or the context menu handles.

options.text: You cannot open the source; you must copy the link and paste it into your browser.
options.explanation: Notion provides a built-in feature to jump directly to the source from within the embed.

questions.question: On Enterprise Plans, what specific permissions can workspace owners restrict to the page level?

options.text: The ability to add connections and decide who can connect or disconnect integrations at the page level.
options.explanation: The article explicitly lists this as one of the two things that can be restricted to workspace owners on Enterprise plans.

options.text: The ability to delete pages within the workspace.
options.explanation: The article does not mention restricting page deletion; it specifically mentions restricting integrations and security compliance permissions.

options.text: The ability to change the page template or style.
options.explanation: Template and style changes are not mentioned as restricted permissions for Enterprise owners in this context.

options.text: The ability to embed external content from specific domains.
options.explanation: While embedding is possible, the specific restriction mentioned for Enterprise owners is regarding the management of connections and integrations.

questions.question: How can you manage your personal account authentications for connected apps?

options.text: Go to Settings in the sidebar → Connections.
options.explanation: The article instructs users to go to 'Settings in the sidebar → Connections' to see all apps they have authenticated or are available.

options.text: Go to the top right corner of the browser window and click Account Settings.
options.explanation: While there is a general account settings area, the specific location for managing app connections and authentications is the sidebar Settings menu.

options.text: Click 'Show all' in the top right corner of any Notion page.
options.explanation: 'Show all' is a feature inside the Connections menu to see additional app options, not the main location to manage authentications.

options.text: Manage these settings in the specific page where the app was connected.
options.explanation: Account authentications are managed at the user/Settings level, not within the specific page context.

questions.question: Which two methods can be used to embed content from your Google Drive into a Notion page?

options.text: Clicking the '+' and selecting Google Drive, or typing '/drive' and pressing enter.
options.explanation: The article explicitly lists these two methods: clicking the + and choosing Google Drive from the menu, or typing /drive.

options.text: Dragging and dropping files from a browser tab onto the page.
options.explanation: Notion does not support dragging files from a browser tab directly into the page to create an embed; you must use the specific Notion menu.

options.text: Pasting a direct link to the file and selecting 'Create Embed'.
options.explanation: You can paste a URL, but the method described involves selecting 'Browse Google Drive' or using the /drive command to search.

options.text: Using the 'Import' button in the sidebar and selecting 'Google Drive'.
options.explanation: There is no 'Import' button mentioned in the context of embedding Google Drive content.
